title: Sally Returns

jokes.text: Tickle tickle tickle! / Hey Bret, how come you don't tickle me anymore?
jokes.setup: Cold open: Jemaine and Bret are apparently tickling a girl. Seems like innocent fun.
jokes.punchline: The girl complains that Bret doesn't tickle her anymore — implying an ongoing intimate tickle routine the audience is dropped into mid-situation.
jokes.explanation: The cold open subverts expectations by normalizing a weirdly intimate three-person tickle scenario as though it's perfectly domestic. The specificity of 'how come you don't tickle me anymore' implies routine and history, which is funnier than the act itself.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Tickle tickle tickle! Hey Bret, how come you don't tickle me anymore?

jokes.text: Oh, I'm just joking. Tickle tickle.
jokes.setup: The girl asks Bret why he doesn't tickle her — implying exclusion from the tickle dynamic.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine says 'Oh, I'm just joking' — then immediately tickles her anyway, revealing he wasn't joking at all. The deflation and reinflation happen instantly.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — the joke is in the instant contradiction of 'I'm just joking' followed by 'Tickle tickle.' Jemaine's deadpan delivery of this rapid reversal is classic FotC tonal control.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm not even ticklish.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, I'm faking it.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah she's faking it as well, I think.

jokes.text: Yeah. What are you up to today, Jemaine? / Uh, I don't know. Actually I'm really bored. / Come on, man, you got to get out of here. / I'm trying to get onto first base.
jokes.setup: Bret is trying to romance a girl while Jemaine just… exists in the same space.
jokes.punchline: Bret admits mid-conversation that he's 'trying to get to first base' — not to Jemaine privately, but as a casual statement in front of the girl, making it both honest and utterly unsexy.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Bret's total lack of game — announcing his romantic intentions in the third person to a roommate while the girl is present. The phrase 'first base' is childish and earnest in a way that's very Bret-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: You were robots.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We were just dressed up as robots.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you were robots.

jokes.text: I'm not even ticklish. / Aren't you? / No, I'm faking it. / Really? / Yeah she's faking it as well, I think.
jokes.setup: Jemaine reveals he's been faking his ticklishness, then casually speculates the girl is faking hers too — in front of her.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's detached third-party assessment of the girl's emotional performance while she's present is deeply awkward and perfectly delivered.
jokes.explanation: This is a quietly excellent moment. Jemaine's socially oblivious act of analyzing the girl's performance to Bret — as if she isn't there — is deeply character-specific. The girl's indignant 'Oh that's not fair, I'm really ticklish' perfectly caps it. Strong character integration, economical, and earns its awkward laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So, you got a boyfriend at the moment?

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: Me? No. You?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Me? I don't have a boyfriend.

jokes.text: Hey, I saw your music video online. 'The year 2000, the year 2000.' Well, that was you, right? Affirmative.
jokes.setup: Sally makes small talk about seeing Jemaine's band's music video.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine replies 'Affirmative' — a stilted, robotic word that inadvertently echoes the robot video she just mentioned. Whether intentional or not in-character, it lands as an absurd verbal tic.
jokes.explanation: Single-word delivery of 'Affirmative' in response to a casual question is classic Jemaine deadpan — he's unconsciously mirroring the robot persona from the video. The economy is excellent; one word does the work of five.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You can't tame the J-Dog.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: What's the J-Dog?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, that's me, I'm the J-Dog.

jokes.text: You were robots. / We were just dressed up as robots. / Yeah, but you were robots. / Yeah, just, you know, acting as robots... / No, yeah, no, I... I know you weren't actually robots.
jokes.setup: Sally describes Jemaine and Bret as robots in their video; Jemaine corrects her; Sally restates it; they go in circles.
jokes.punchline: The loop of 'you were robots / we were dressed as robots / yeah but you were robots' becomes its own comedic object — a conversation going nowhere, exposing Sally's mild dim-wittedness and Jemaine's helpless politeness.
jokes.explanation: The repetition engine here is perfectly calibrated — three beats of the same misunderstanding, with Sally's final 'No, yeah, no, I know you weren't actually robots' suggesting she half-understands but will never fully commit. Economical, absurdist, and very FotC.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, it's street language. You know, you just take the first letter of your name and you put 'Dog' on the end of it and all the other dogs sort of respect you.

jokes.text: No, not me. You can't tame the J-Dog. / What's the J-Dog? / Uh, well, that's me, I'm the J-Dog.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is asked if he has a girlfriend and tries to present himself as a free spirit.
jokes.punchline: He deploys the self-given nickname 'J-Dog' with total seriousness, then has to explain it when Sally doesn't know what it means.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's self-mythology as 'the J-Dog' is brilliant character comedy — a man with no evident street cred attempting hip-hop cool. The system he explains ('you take the first letter of your name and put Dog on the end') is so earnestly articulated it becomes its own joke. Sally's response 'I thought it was from a kid's book' is the perfect puncture.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: I thought it was from a kid's book or something.

jokes.text: Well, it's street language. You know, you just take the first letter of your name and you put 'Dog' on the end of it and all the other dogs sort of respect you.
jokes.setup: Jemaine explains the J-Dog nickname system.
jokes.punchline: 'All the other dogs sort of respect you' — the hierarchical dog-respect economy described in complete seriousness by a New Zealand folk musician on a Manhattan street.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. Jemaine's deadpan elaboration of a completely made-up 'street' social structure — delivered as genuine insider knowledge — achieves the rare feat of being both absurdist and totally plausible for this character. The phrase 'all the other dogs sort of respect you' has just the right hedge ('sort of') to feel like Jemaine half-doubts it himself. Genuinely original and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, the thing about the J-Dog is you can't put a leash on the J-Dog.

jokes.text: I thought it was from a kid's book or something.
jokes.setup: Jemaine has explained the J-Dog system with great seriousness.
jokes.punchline: Sally's flat 'I thought it was from a kid's book' undercuts his street-cred posturing and accidentally nails something true — it does sound like a children's character.
jokes.explanation: Perfect comic puncture — seven words that destroy Jemaine's entire J-Dog mythology. The 'or something' at the end makes it even better, implying Sally isn't even sure it's worth identifying. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Because it's Wednesday / And Wednesday night is the night that we usually make love

jokes.text: No, the thing about the J-Dog is you can't put a leash on the J-Dog.
jokes.setup: Sally has punctured the J-Dog concept; Jemaine doubles down.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine persists with the 'can't be tamed' mythology, using literal dog language ('leash') while claiming freedom — the metaphor swallows itself.
jokes.explanation: The joke escalates by pushing deeper into the dog metaphor — 'you can't put a leash on the J-Dog' is both a pride statement and an accidental admission that J-Dog is, in fact, a dog. Jemaine's commitment to the bit in the face of obvious skepticism is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Tuesday night is the night that we usually go to your mother's place / And I teach her how to use the video machine again

jokes.text: Oh. (beat after Sally says she really does want to settle down)
jokes.setup: Jemaine has been performing his 'J-Dog can't be tamed' persona; Sally sincerely says she wants to settle down.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's deflated 'Oh.' — a single syllable of a man watching his cool persona dissolve in real time as the girl he likes expresses exactly what he said he didn't want.
jokes.explanation: The single 'Oh.' perfectly cashes out the J-Dog sequence. Jemaine has talked himself into a corner and the monosyllabic response captures everything — regret, recalculation, defeat. The silence before it is as funny as the word itself. Exceptional economy, strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're not too tired from your afterwork social netball team practice

jokes.text: Business Time — the full song
jokes.setup: A soul/R&B sex song beginning with the classic trappings of the genre — 'Girl, tonight we're gonna make love' — performed with total sincerity.
jokes.punchline: The song systematically demolishes romantic cliché by replacing it with the mundane reality of a long-term relationship: Wednesday is lovemaking night by default, 'foreplay' includes sorting recycling, the T-shirt has a stain from Team Building Exercise '99, the act takes two minutes because he's 'so intense,' and 'Is that it?!' is reinterpreted as enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: One of the great comedy songs in television history. The genius is structural: it maintains the exact sonic vocabulary of earnest 70s soul while subverting every romantic lyric with granular domestic detail. Each verse escalates the anti-romance: the schedule ('Wednesday night'), the context ('nothing good on TV'), the foreplay ('the recycling — which isn't part of the foreplay, but it's still very important'), the wardrobe ('Team Building Exercise '99'), the duration ('two minutes... because I'm so intense'), the socks ('that's why they're called business socks'). Every detail is specific enough to feel observed from life. The self-delusion is the key — Jemaine genuinely believes this is sexy. Scores 9+ because it became a genuine cultural touchstone, is quoted constantly, and the craft is exceptional at every level.

dialogue.line: You turn to me and say something sexy like / 'I might go to bed, I have to work in the morning'

jokes.text: Tuesday night is the night that we usually go to your mother's place / And I teach her how to use the video machine again
jokes.setup: The song is establishing weekly intimacy schedules.
jokes.punchline: Tuesday is not a romantic alternative — it's visiting the girlfriend's mother to teach her to use a video machine. The specificity of 'again' is devastating.
jokes.explanation: The word 'again' is the entire joke — it implies this is an ongoing, recurring obligation that has replaced romance as a domestic ritual. Surgical economy within an already excellent song. Could stand alone as a joke outside the song context.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I know what you're trying to say, baby / You're trying to say 'Ah, yeah / It's business time'

jokes.text: You're not too tired from your afterwork social netball team practice
jokes.setup: Cataloguing the specific 'right conditions' for Wednesday lovemaking.
jokes.punchline: 'Afterwork social netball team practice' — the phrase is doing triple work: it's extremely specific (not just 'exercise' but netball), 'social' signals amateur non-serious sport, and 'practice' implies they're not even good at it. The rhythm of the syllables is itself funny.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely in the taxonomic specificity. A less crafted writer would say 'you're not too tired.' Instead we get the full NZ domestic bureaucracy of 'afterwork social netball team practice.' Every word adds. Strong.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The next thing you know we're in the bathroom brushing our teeth / That's all part of it, that's foreplay

jokes.text: There's nothing good on TV / Mm, conditions are perfect / For making love
jokes.setup: Cataloguing the 'perfect conditions' for romance.
jokes.punchline: The final condition — literally nothing better to do — is the ultimate romantic deflation. 'Conditions are perfect' delivered like a meteorologist about 'nothing good on TV.'
jokes.explanation: The structure here is perfect: the setup lists legitimate conditions (not tired, etc.) and then lands on 'nothing good on TV' as the clinching argument. 'Conditions are perfect' sung with genuine enthusiasm about the TV schedule is one of the best individual lines in the song.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Then you go sort out the recycling / Which isn't part of the foreplay / But it's still very important

jokes.text: You turn to me and say something sexy like / 'I might go to bed, I have to work in the morning' / I know what you're trying to say, baby
jokes.setup: The song sets up a classic 'she says something sexy' moment.
jokes.punchline: The 'something sexy' is a literal wind-down statement about needing sleep for work — and Jemaine interprets it as a come-on. The cognitive dissonance is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is perfectly structured: 'say something sexy like' primes the audience for something lurid, then delivers the most mundane possible sentence. 'I know what you're trying to say, baby' as the response is the character in full self-delusion mode — he has decoded this as invitation. One of the song's best beats.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You're wearing that same old ugly baggy T-shirt / With the stain on it that you got / From that team-building exercise / You did for your old work several years ago / Team Building Exercise '99

jokes.text: Then you go sort out the recycling / Which isn't part of the foreplay / But it's still very important
jokes.setup: Having established that brushing teeth IS foreplay, the song continues the foreplay sequence.
jokes.punchline: The recycling gets its own parenthetical caveat — 'which isn't part of the foreplay, but it's still very important' — the responsible-citizen asterisk in the middle of a sex song.
jokes.explanation: The recycling caveat is one of the funniest lines in the song. The careful bureaucratic distinction between what counts as foreplay and what doesn't — delivered earnestly, as if correcting a potential misunderstanding — is perfectly observed. The civic pride of 'but it's still very important' makes it transcendent. This is the song's most purely FotC moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I take off my clothes, but I trip over my jeans / Because I'm still wearing my shoes / But it's okay because I turn it all into a sexy dance

jokes.text: You're wearing that same old ugly baggy T-shirt / With the stain on it that you got / From that team-building exercise / You did for your old work several years ago / Team Building Exercise '99
jokes.setup: The song has moved to the bedroom for the romantic crescendo.
jokes.punchline: Instead of lingerie or anything sexy, she's in the T-shirt from Team Building Exercise '99 — the specificity of naming it, giving it a year, and noting it's from an 'old work' is a masterclass in comedic detail. The shirt gets its own lyrical monument.
jokes.explanation: The entire joke is in the specificity spiral: not just a T-shirt, an ugly one, from a team-building exercise, at an old job, several years ago. 'Team Building Exercise '99' as a capitalized lyrical phrase transforms it into a kind of folk monument to romantic disappointment. Brilliant observed humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The next thing you know, I'm wearing absolutely nothing / Except for my socks / And you know when I'm down to just my socks / What time it is / It's business time

jokes.text: I take off my clothes, but I trip over my jeans / Because I'm still wearing my shoes / But it's okay because I turn it all into a sexy dance
jokes.setup: The seduction sequence is escalating toward intimacy.
jokes.punchline: He trips over his own jeans because of his shoes, then salvages it by declaring the fall a 'sexy dance.' The self-saving narrative spin is perfectly in character.
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy here is entirely verbal — the audience imagines the stumble, then the recovery. 'But it's okay because I turn it all into a sexy dance' is the exact self-delusion move of the character: when reality fails, reframe it. The casual 'it's okay' before the reveal is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That's why they're called business socks, ooh

jokes.text: The next thing you know, I'm wearing absolutely nothing / Except for my socks / And you know when I'm down to just my socks / What time it is / It's business time
jokes.setup: The undressing sequence approaches nakedness.
jokes.punchline: The socks remain. And the socks are the signal. 'Down to my socks' as the erotic threshold is both physically accurate (most men do this) and completely unromantic.
jokes.explanation: This is the song's most iconic moment. The revelation that socks-only is his erotic signature — and that she recognizes it — and that this triggers the chorus — is both structurally perfect and genuinely observed. 'That's why they're called business socks' is the pinnacle: a pseudo-etymology that makes absolute no sense but feels completely inevitable. One of the great comedy song payoffs.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Making love for two / I'm making love for two minutes / When it's with me, you only need two minutes, girl / Because I'm so intense

jokes.text: Making love for two minutes / When it's with me, you only need two minutes, girl / Because I'm so intense
jokes.setup: The song approaches its sexual climax.
jokes.punchline: Two minutes. And the self-justification — 'because I'm so intense' — is the perfect cap. The reframe of inadequacy as a superpower.
jokes.explanation: 'Because I'm so intense' is maybe the most perfectly constructed line in the song's second half. The character turns a deficiency into a boast with complete sincerity. The follow-up 'Two minutes in heaven is better than one minute in heaven' — a desperate reframing — escalates it further.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Two minutes in heaven is better than one minute in heaven

jokes.text: You turn to me and say something sexy like / 'Is that it?!' / I know what you're trying to say, girl / You're trying to say 'Ah, yeah, that's it'
jokes.setup: Callback structure mirrors the earlier 'say something sexy like' beat.
jokes.punchline: 'Is that it?!' — clearly a disappointed question — is interpreted as 'Ah, yeah, that's it' — a declaration of satisfaction. The delusional misread is the song's emotional core.
jokes.explanation: This is the emotional and comedic climax of the song. The callback structure is exact — same setup, different punchline — and the misread of 'Is that it?!' as approval is both devastating about the character and structurally perfect. The audience has been set up by the first 'something sexy' beat and gets a much bigger payoff here.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You turn to me and say something sexy like / 'Is that it?!' / I know what you're trying to say, girl / You're trying to say 'Ah, yeah, that's it'

jokes.text: Then you tell me you want some more / Well, uh, I'm not surprised / But I am quite sleepy
jokes.setup: After 'not being surprised' she wants more — the song seems to be building to a continuation.
jokes.punchline: 'But I am quite sleepy' — the complete collapse of sexual bravado in five words. Business hours are indeed over.
jokes.explanation: The 'I'm not surprised' bravado immediately undercut by 'But I am quite sleepy' is the song's final character beat — and it's perfect. The 'Business hours are over, baby' coda follows naturally. The whole song has been building to this admission and it earns it completely.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Then you tell me you want some more / Well, uh, I'm not surprised / But I am quite sleepy

jokes.text: Well, here's the doghouse. / Thanks for walking me home, Sally.
jokes.setup: Jemaine walks Sally home, implying a romantic atmosphere.
jokes.punchline: He introduces his home as 'the doghouse' — doubling down on the J-Dog mythology with no apparent self-awareness about what 'doghouse' typically means (being in trouble).
jokes.explanation: Callback to the J-Dog persona — Jemaine has named his apartment 'the doghouse' in keeping with his self-mythology. The irony that he's literally in the doghouse (romantically) adds an extra layer the character doesn't register.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Business hours are over / Baby

jokes.text: Um, should I come up for a cup of coffee? / What are you... are you serious? / Oh, in my... up there? / Yeah. / I mean, obviously if you're busy... / No, I'm not busy at all
jokes.setup: Sally makes a classic 'coffee' come-on.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's total inability to process good fortune — 'What are you... are you serious?' 'Oh, in my... up there?' — conveys a man who has so thoroughly convinced himself of failure that success breaks his brain.
jokes.explanation: Classic Jemaine character beat — he's so used to failure that he malfunctions when something works. The fragmented 'in my... up there?' is a great scripted approximation of flustered speech.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, here's the doghouse.

jokes.text: Wait, you're not still living with Bret, are you? / Maybe. Yes. / Actually, definitely yes.
jokes.setup: Jemaine was about to score; Sally asks about his living situation.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's three-stage confession — 'Maybe. Yes. / Actually, definitely yes.' — is a miniature journey from denial to reluctant acceptance. The 'Actually, definitely yes' escalation is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The three beats of escalating admission — 'Maybe / Yes / Actually, definitely yes' — compress a character arc into six words. The 'Actually' implies he momentarily tried to maintain the 'maybe' before reality collapsed in. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: I can't believe you're still living with Bret. / Jemaine, you gotta get your own place. / You need your own space.

jokes.text: Bret, I'm moving out. / I've decided I need my own space. / It's time to take life a little more seriously, you know? / It's time for me to look each day in the eye and say 'Hey, Jemaine you're... you're doing this for you.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine delivers a grand speech about self-actualization and independence.
jokes.punchline: The reason is immediately undercut by context — he's moving out to get laid. The speech trails off with the awkward third-person self-address 'Hey, Jemaine you're... you're doing this for you' — the ellipsis suggesting he isn't quite sure what he's doing this for.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is the gap between the grandiloquent framing ('look each day in the eye') and the real motivation (Sally won't come upstairs). The stumbling third-person speech is nicely observed. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, I'm moving out. I've decided I need my own space. It's time to take life a little more seriously, you know? It's time for me to look each day in the eye and say 'Hey, Jemaine you're... you're doing this for you.'

jokes.text: Is it because of the towel? / What towel? / Because I used your towel and I got athlete's foot.
jokes.setup: Bret is trying to identify the real reason Jemaine is moving out.
jokes.punchline: He used Jemaine's towel and got athlete's foot — and he's bringing this up as a potential grievance Jemaine has against him, when in fact it's Bret who was harmed.
jokes.explanation: The logical inversion is perfect — Bret presents himself as the wrongdoer ('I used your towel and got athlete's foot') without realizing the direction of harm is reversed. The joke is Bret's total confusion about cause and effect in interpersonal grievances.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What's that, man?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh didn't you hear that?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What did you say? I wasn't listening.

jokes.text: Is it my mold farm? / Is it aspergillus fumigatus?
jokes.setup: Bret continues listing potential grievances Jemaine has with him.
jokes.punchline: Bret has a 'mold farm' in their shared apartment, and he knows the specific species by its Latin name — suggesting dedicated mycological attention.
jokes.explanation: 'Mold farm' alone is funny — a man with a deliberate mold cultivation project in a shared NYC apartment. But 'Is it aspergillus fumigatus?' elevates it significantly: Bret knows the Latin name of his molds, suggesting connoisseurship. The precision is the joke. Genuinely original character detail.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Was a perfectly round hickey. She had a perfectly round mouth anyways... It's not exactly the same size as a vacuum cleaner tube...

jokes.text: When we were at school and you said you got a hickey from Judy Bailey, I told everyone it was the vacuum cleaner.
jokes.setup: Bret continues escalating the list of possible grievances, going further back in time.
jokes.punchline: We learn Bret betrayed Jemaine in school by exposing his Judy Bailey hickey as vacuum-cleaner-related — a very specific, very old wound surfaced at exactly the wrong moment.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Judy Bailey' and the vacuum cleaner defense — and the implication that Bret did this deliberately rather than accidentally — is a great bit of character backstory comedy. The detail 'it was a perfectly round hickey / she had a perfectly round mouth' escalates it further.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Was it because of the towel?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Because I used your towel and I got athlete's foot.

jokes.text: Well, it's not exactly the same size as a vacuum cleaner tube... / It's not that, it's not that.
jokes.setup: The Judy Bailey hickey debate escalates to a forensic analysis.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine defending the size of Judy Bailey's mouth relative to a vacuum cleaner tube — then insisting this isn't why he's moving out — is a perfect comic derailment of the main plot.
jokes.explanation: The escalation into mouth-size forensics is delightful. 'Not exactly the same size as a vacuum cleaner tube' is Jemaine's hapless attempt to defend his romantic history, and the speed at which Bret moves on ('Is it because I drank all the orange juice?') makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is it my mold farm? / Are you sick of my mold farm? / Is it aspergillus fumigatus?

jokes.text: Is it something psychological? Because I put you down in front of other people? / He wouldn't know, because he's got no idea.
jokes.setup: Bret is self-examining for psychological cruelty.
jokes.punchline: He immediately demonstrates the exact behavior he's asking about — 'he wouldn't know, because he's got no idea' — said presumably to no one in particular (or to camera), while accusing himself of the habit.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed self-undermining moment — Bret asks if his putting Jemaine down might be the problem, then immediately puts him down. The casualness of it ('he wouldn't know, because he's got no idea') suggests complete lack of self-awareness, which is the joke. Strong structural irony.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: When we were at school and you said you got a hickey from Judy Bailey, I told everyone it was the vacuum cleaner. Was a perfectly round hickey. She had a perfectly round mouth anyways...

jokes.text: Is it because sometimes I'll sneak in when you're sleeping and give you a little kiss on the cheek?
jokes.setup: The list of Bret's possible offenses has escalated from towels to mold to psychological abuse.
jokes.punchline: The creepiest entry in the list arrives last and most casually — 'sneak in when you're sleeping and give you a little kiss on the cheek' — presented as if it might be a minor faux pas rather than a significant boundary violation.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of this list has been building perfectly and this is its peak. The delivery of 'a little kiss on the cheek' as though it's comparable to the orange juice grievance is the joke — Bret's complete inability to register the severity of what he's describing. 'A little kiss' is doing enormous work.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is it because sometimes I'll sneak in when you're sleeping and give you a little kiss on the cheek?

jokes.text: Is it because I ask too many questions? / No. / Was it something subtle? / Yes it's subtle, Bret.
jokes.setup: Bret has been asking an escalating list of questions.
jokes.punchline: 'Is it because I ask too many questions?' — meta-comment on the scene itself as the final question in an infinite series, immediately followed by 'Was it something subtle?' proving he cannot stop.
jokes.explanation: The meta-question 'Is it because I ask too many questions?' is the perfect acknowledgment of the running gag — and then 'Was it something subtle?' immediately reinstates it. The show is aware of what it's doing and lands the structural joke cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is it because I ask too many questions?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Was it something subtle?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes it's subtle, Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is it because I eat too loudly?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: Is it because I eat too loudly? / Yes. / It is? / Yeah. / I knew it.
jokes.setup: Jemaine finally gives Bret an answer — 'Yes' — after all the impossible questioning.
jokes.punchline: The real reason is a cover story — eating loudly — but Bret's 'I knew it' suggests he's been worried about this all along. The false catharsis is complete.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the whole question sequence — Jemaine just picks one to make Bret stop — is satisfying. 'I knew it' suggests Bret has been guilty about this for some time, which adds a small emotional wrinkle to the comedy.

jokes.setup: The eating loudly grievance has been aired.
jokes.punchline: The escalation of 'deafening / so deafening' applied to eating, combined with Bret's genuine claim to eat with his mouth shut and Jemaine's baffled 'I don't know how you do it' — treating a basic human skill as a mystery.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in taking the accusation seriously enough to debate technique. Jemaine's 'I don't know how you do it' treats closed-mouth eating as an advanced skill, which is a nice absurdist touch.

jokes.setup: Murray takes attendance at the band meeting.
jokes.punchline: Murray calls his own name, confirms his own presence, then laments the pointlessness of his own line — a meta-moment where he acknowledges the formality of the ritual makes no sense.
jokes.explanation: Murray's self-awareness about the absurdity of his own management ritual — calling roll when he's the only one holding the meeting and he's always there — is a great character moment. 'I don't know why I bothered with my line' is Murray at his most self-defeating.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: So I'm proud to announce that as of this morning we're the official owners of three stars... intergalactic real estate.

jokes.text: Intergalactic real estate. / All right, so there's mine, Murray. / Bret, yours is up here. / And Jemaine, you're on the other side of the nebula there. / But that's as close as we could get the three that were available.
jokes.setup: Murray has invested band funds in 'the way to go' — property.
jokes.punchline: He has bought three stars in intergalactic space and named them, presenting a NASA photograph as a property portfolio. The three stars are on different sides of a nebula but 'that's as close as we could get.'
jokes.explanation: The reveal of 'intergalactic real estate' is a superb misdirect — Murray's earnest framing of star-buying as a legitimate property investment is perfectly in character. The concern about them being on 'different sides of the nebula' as though this is a commute problem is wonderful. The naming ('Murrayland, Planet Bret, Planet Jemaine') escalates it.

jokes.punchline: The casual admission that he's been 'creaming a bit off the top' of band income — a phrase that in any other context describes embezzlement — delivered as good news.
jokes.explanation: The unintentional admission of financial impropriety through innocent vocabulary is a good Murray moment — he has no idea 'creaming off the top' sounds terrible. Functional character comedy.

jokes.explanation: 'Planet Murray was already taken' implies someone else got there first in the star-naming economy — a beautiful absurdist detail suggesting competition in the celestial real estate market. 'Welcome to Murrayland' as a tourism slogan for a named star is pitch-perfect Murray.

jokes.explanation: Murray's coal fantasy is excellent — his vision of interstellar wealth is specifically the most Victorian, least exciting resource. 'Oh my God, it's coal everywhere' delivered with genuine wonder is a peak Murray moment. The escalation from 'natural resources' to the disappointingly mundane specificity of coal is the structural engine.

jokes.explanation: 'A lot of things would be nice, Bret, particularly if they were real' is one of the episode's best lines — and the irony that Murray's own coal planet is equally fictional makes it self-undermining. Highly quotable, efficient, and character-specific to Murray's pompous pragmatism.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: And that's... that's multipurpose.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You can use... use that around the house.

jokes.text: I made you this for your birthday. / Aw. / It's a butterfly. / I sat on it in the subway. / Oh, you shouldn't have. / But I also got you some glue. / Oh. / And that's... that's multipurpose. / You can use... use that around the house.
jokes.setup: Bret presents Sally with a glass butterfly for her birthday.
jokes.punchline: He sat on it in the subway and it broke — but he bought glue as a recovery gift. The escalation from broken gift to glue to 'that's multipurpose, you can use that around the house' is a beautiful comedic collapse.
jokes.explanation: The arc from gift to destruction to glue redemption is perfectly structured. 'You can use that around the house' as the practical pitch for multipurpose glue as a birthday present is a great escalation. Bret's earnestness throughout makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: See, that's me, and that's some really weird kind of wolf.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, it's difficult to draw a wolf, so I used a picture of myself and then adjusted it.

jokes.text: Hey, did you see this amazing painting Bret made me? / A painting? / See, that's me, and that's some really weird kind of wolf. / Well, it's difficult to draw a wolf, so I used a picture of myself and then adjusted it.
jokes.setup: Sally reveals Bret also made her a painting.
jokes.punchline: The painting is of Sally and a wolf — but the wolf is clearly Bret with adjustments, because he found wolves hard to draw. The artistic compromise is both lazily practical and deeply weird.
jokes.explanation: Bret's solution to not being able to draw a wolf — use a self-portrait and 'adjust it' — is a perfect character beat. The result is a creepy painting that Sally admits is 'kind of creepy, but it's pretty.' The meta-absurdism of a man drawing himself as a wolf for a girl he likes is both funny and weirdly poignant.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: I got engaged. Mark and I are getting married.
Who's Mark?

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: Mark, he was my boyfriend and then we broke up and now he's my fiancé.

jokes.text: Is it Mark's birthday as well? / No! / I got engaged. Mark and I are getting married.
jokes.setup: Sally announces she has news; Bret guesses it's another birthday.
jokes.punchline: Sally is engaged — catastrophic news for both Bret and Jemaine — delivered against Bret's hilariously understated guess ('is it Mark's birthday as well?').
jokes.explanation: Bret's guess — 'is it Mark's birthday as well?' — is a lovely bit of misdirection. The engagement reveal is devastating to the characters and the comic timing of Sally's emphatic 'No!' is good.

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: He bought me a BMW.

jokes.text: Who's Mark? / Mark, he was my boyfriend and then we broke up and now he's my fiancé. / He bought me a BMW.
jokes.setup: Sally announces her engagement.
jokes.punchline: The BMW detail — offered as a capstone to the relationship summary — positions the car as a key romantic factor, with Sally's delivery suggesting it's at least partially the reason for the reconciliation.
jokes.explanation: The BMW as romantic turning point confirms Sally's shallowness (referenced earlier in the episode) while Sally remains oblivious. Competent, functional, not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: He's little, he's like a pixie. Come on.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Brettie, aye, ohh yes!

dialogue.speaker: Sally
dialogue.line: Isn't he strong?

jokes.text: Go on and show them your abs. / No... / No, go show them. / Rock hard. / Hey you guys want to see them? / Yes. / No, no thank you, Mark.
jokes.setup: Mark (the Australian fiancé) is being introduced.
jokes.punchline: Sally pushes Mark to display his abs; Jemaine's 'Yes' is immediately overridden by his own 'No, no thank you, Mark' — he wants to see them and is ashamed of wanting to see them.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine saying 'Yes' then immediately walking it back to 'No, no thank you, Mark' is a great quick beat — involuntary attraction to male fitness overridden by social self-consciousness. Bret saying 'Yes' without any such reversal adds a nice contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: But I don't just love and need you / I love and need and want you too / I don't just love and need you / I love and need and want you too too

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I love, need and want you too too too

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There's too many of those

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's just ridiculous

jokes.text: Pick up Bret. / No, I can't... / Just lift him up. He's little, he's like a pixie. Come on. / Brettie, aye, ohh yes! / Isn't he strong?
jokes.setup: Mark's physical prowess is being demonstrated.
jokes.punchline: Sally calls Bret 'a pixie' and has her fiancé lift him — a complete infantilization of one of her two suitors, while the other watches. Mark's 'Brettie, aye' is Australian bro-energy at its most patronizing.
jokes.explanation: The physical humiliation of Bret being lifted like a child by his rival — described as 'a pixie' by the woman both men love — is good cringe comedy. Sally's complete obliviousness makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah yeah, she gets it... Stop cockblocking me

jokes.text: What's that? / Oh, that's a broken butterfly.
jokes.setup: Mark notices Bret's destroyed gift.
jokes.punchline: Bret's flat 'Oh, that's a broken butterfly' — delivered as if this is a normal category of object — is perfect deadpan deflation.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. 'A broken butterfly' as a matter-of-fact category — not 'a butterfly I broke' but 'a broken butterfly,' as if it came that way — is a lovely small moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: But maybe Mark will be involved / In an accident and you'll get / A life insurance payment of half a mil / It's not about the money / But it could set us up financially

jokes.text: Sally, I Love You — the full song (both characters singing simultaneously at a party)
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine, having both lost Sally to Mark, perform a simultaneously-composed love song for her — at her engagement party.
jokes.punchline: The song reveals they are competing within the same musical number, accuse each other of copying, acknowledge Bret has a girlfriend, discuss the possibility of Mark dying in an accident and Sally collecting life insurance, and devolve into direct in-song arguments about whose declaration is more legitimate.
jokes.explanation: The conceit of two men composing separate love songs that merge into one argumentative duet is brilliantly conceived. Each revelation escalates: they're competing in real time, then Jemaine accuses Bret of copying, then the Mark insurance speculation ('maybe Mark will be involved in an accident and you'll get a life insurance payment of half a mil'), then the Coco subplot emerges mid-song, then they start arguing about whose declaration of love is more impressive. The song maintains musical sincerity while being structurally chaotic — the form and content are perfectly mismatched. Strong character integration because only these two specific characters would have this specific argument in this specific way.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Um, even sometimes a little bit more / Than my current girlfriend

jokes.text: # The only thing stopping you from being with me / Is that you don't want to be with me / It's the same with me, except with me #
jokes.setup: The love song is expressing romantic longing.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine identifies the single obstacle to their relationship — 'you don't want to be with me' — delivered as a lyric with complete earnest self-awareness. Bret's 'it's the same with me, except with me' is a perfect echo that somehow makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional lyric. 'The only thing stopping you from being with me is that you don't want to be with me' is a perfect tautological summation of unrequited love — presented not as pathos but as a solvable equation. 'It's the same with me, except with me' is the ideal echo: it adds nothing to the logic while multiplying the sadness. Near-perfect economy and originality.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, he's basically said just the same thing / I think he's been looking over at my bit of paper / Except for the girlfriend bit

jokes.text: # Yeah yeah, she gets it... Stop cockblocking me #
jokes.setup: Bret has been singing alongside Jemaine in the same love song.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine breaks the lyrical register entirely to tell Bret to stop cockblocking him — mid-song, at a party, to the woman he's serenading — blending the raw complaint with the musical format.
jokes.explanation: The sudden shift from lyrical sentiment to 'Stop cockblocking me' is a perfectly executed tonal rupture. The word is so incongruous in a song of yearning that it creates a genuine shock laugh. Delivered as part of the melody, suggesting it was planned, which makes it weirder.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Because you and me we were meant to be
Bret's got a girlfriend

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, but Sally and me, we were meant to be
Bret, you got a girlfriend

jokes.text: # I can't help / But think that now you're engaged / We're drifting apart #
jokes.setup: Sally has just announced her engagement.
jokes.punchline: The understatement of 'we're drifting apart' as the consequence of her engagement — rather than 'you're engaged to someone else and I never had a chance' — is comedically precise.
jokes.explanation: 'We're drifting apart' as an understatement for 'you got engaged to someone else' is very FotC — the gap between the real situation and the mild language used to describe it is the whole joke.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, Coco told me to tell you you're dumped.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh... oh, sorry, man.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: # It won't or it will / But maybe Mark will be involved / In an accident and you'll get / A life insurance payment of half a mil / It's not about the money / But it could set us up financially / If you came back to me #
jokes.setup: Jemaine is musing about the future of love.
jokes.punchline: He speculates, mid-love-song, about Mark dying in an accident and Sally collecting half a million in life insurance — then clarifies 'it's not about the money' — the financial planning of a man proposing through tragedy.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The transition from sincere love-song sentiment to actuarial death-planning — 'maybe Mark will be involved in an accident and you'll get a life insurance payment of half a mil' — is a completely unexpected tonal lurch. 'It's not about the money, but it could set us up financially' is the perfect qualifier — simultaneously dark, practical, and oblivious to how terrible it sounds. The 'half a mil' figure suggests he's already thought about this. Exceptional originality and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She said to say that you've been ignoring her / and that you're clearly still in love with Sally, / which I agree with, actually...

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She said you'd say that and she said to say / it is true and you know it.

jokes.text: # Sally, I wrote you this song so I could tell you how much I love you / Quite a lot, actually / Um, even sometimes a little bit more / Than my current girlfriend #
jokes.setup: Jemaine is declaring his love for Sally.
jokes.punchline: The quantification — 'quite a lot, actually / um, even sometimes a little bit more than my current girlfriend' — reveals (1) he has a current girlfriend he hasn't mentioned, (2) 'quite a lot' is his love declaration, and (3) it's only 'sometimes' more than girlfriend-level.
jokes.explanation: The slow qualification of 'quite a lot / um, even sometimes a little bit more than my current girlfriend' is brilliantly structured. Each hedge undercuts the declaration further. The reveal of a current girlfriend — in a song of devotion to another woman — delivered casually ('than my current girlfriend') is both character-defining and hilarious. Exceptional structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No buts, she said to say.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, did she say we could talk about it?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: She said we are talking about it.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, we're not though are we?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, no.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I don't know what she meant by that.

jokes.text: # Sally, I co-wrote this song to tell you / How much I love you / It's quite a lot, actually / Well, he's basically said just the same thing / I think he's been looking over at my bit of paper / Except for the girlfriend bit #
jokes.setup: Bret's parallel verse mirrors Jemaine's.
jokes.punchline: Bret accuses Jemaine of copying his lyrics mid-song — the meta-awareness that they've written the same love song for the same woman becomes its own comedic subject. 'Except for the girlfriend bit' acknowledges the one thing that makes Jemaine's worse.
jokes.explanation: The song turning into a plagiarism dispute is an excellent structural escalation — the form becomes the joke. 'I think he's been looking over at my bit of paper' implies they physically wrote their songs down, nearby each other, which is both practical and absurd. 'Except for the girlfriend bit' is the perfect precise exception.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's too late. It's over, you bastard.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What, did she call me a bastard?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Uh, no sorry, I added that bit.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, okay.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I got carried away.

jokes.text: # Bret's got a girlfriend / Yeah, but Sally and me, we were meant to be / Bret, you got a girlfriend / Yeah, well, I'd break it off with her if I knew / Sally wanted to be with me / Well just so you know, Sally, unlike Bret / I'm available immediately #
jokes.setup: The song's in-song argument escalates.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's competitive advantage — 'unlike Bret, I'm available immediately' — is both practically accurate and romantically absurd as a selling point. The song has become a live negotiation.
jokes.explanation: 'I'm available immediately' as a romantic sales pitch — the language of job listings applied to love — is a perfect culmination of the song's competitive arc. The directness of it, delivered as a musical lyric to a woman at her engagement party, is peak FotC. Bret's willingness to dump his girlfriend on contingency ('I'd break it off with her if I knew') is equally revealing.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well she told me to do that.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I was supposed to start with that actually, I'm sorry, I forgot.

jokes.text: Oh, Coco told me to tell you you're dumped.
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine are wrapping up the episode.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's girlfriend — barely mentioned — has dumped him via Bret as messenger, and Bret delivers this as an afterthought.
jokes.explanation: The dumping-by-proxy setup is a good callback to the girlfriend reveal in the song. Bret's casual delivery of devastating news is characterful.

jokes.text: She said you're emotionally immature / She said you'd say that and she said to say it is true and you know it. / Oh, but, I mean... / No buts, she said to say.
jokes.setup: Bret relaying Coco's breakup message; Jemaine trying to respond
jokes.punchline: Coco has pre-scripted responses to Jemaine's likely objections — 'she said you'd say that and she said to say it is true' — turning Bret into an automated breakup delivery system
jokes.explanation: Justifying 8.1: Coco anticipating every objection and pre-loading responses through Bret is excellent structural comedy. 'She said you'd say that' covering any denial, 'no buts, she said to say' as robotic script delivery — Bret is a human chatbot running Coco's breakup software. The joke escalates with 'did she say we could talk about it?' / 'She said we are talking about it' / 'Well, we're not though, are we?' / 'Well, no.' — the absurdity that Bret is the conversation while claiming not to be.

jokes.text: She said you'd say that and she said to say / it is true and you know it. / Oh, but, I mean... / No buts, she said to say. / Well, did she say we could talk about it? / She said we are talking about it. / Well, we're not though are we? / Well, no. / I don't know what she meant by that.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is trying to process the breakup through Bret as intermediary.
jokes.punchline: The entire breakup conversation is conducted through Bret relaying Coco's pre-scripted responses — including 'she said we are talking about it' being obviously false as they're talking about whether they're talking about it.
jokes.explanation: The absurdist structure of a breakup mediated entirely through a friend's script is brilliantly conceived. Coco has pre-loaded every response — including 'she said we are talking about it' for when Jemaine asks if they can talk about it — creating a philosophical paradox. 'Well, we're not though are we? / Well, no. / I don't know what she meant by that' is a perfect endpoint. Strong structure and originality.

tmdb_overview: Sally, the ex-girlfriend of both Bret and Jemaine, from the season premiere, returns to further complicate things in the band.
